content:
First note that any “block” of $k$ ones contributes 
\[
f(k)=\underbrace{11\cdots1}_{k\text{ ones}}
=\frac{10^k-1}9.
\]
Since $f(4)=1111>1000$, only blocks of size 1,2,3 can appear.  Write
\[
x\cdot f(1)+y\cdot f(2)+z\cdot f(3)=x+11y+111z=1000,
\]
with $x,y,z\ge0$.  Then the total length is
\[
n=x+2y+3z.
\]
From $x=1000-11y-111z$ we get
\[
n=(1000-11y-111z)+2y+3z=1000-9(y+12z).
\]
Let $k=y+12z$.  One checks easily that as $(y,z)$ range over all non‐negative solutions of $11y+111z\le1000$, $k$ runs through exactly $0,1,2,\dots,108$.  Hence
\[
n=1000-9k,
\quad k=0,1,\dots,108,
\]
i.e.\ 
\[
n\in\{1000,991,982,\dots,28\},
\]
a total of $109$ values.  Thus $a=109$, and the sum of its digits is
\[
1+0+9=10.
\]
Answer: \boxed{10}.
